docs.title: mininpu Compute-Tile ISA Reference
docs.text:
mininpu · Doc 1 of 3 · Locked

Instruction Set Architecture reference for the mininpu matmul/vector accelerator compute tile, version 0.5.0.

ISA 0.5.0 · 15 opcodes · 64-bit word · in-order, no interlock

Spec vs RTL as-built

Spec: ISA 0.5.0 (frozen; amendments tracked in #381) · RTL as-built: 0.4.0 · cutover pending.

This page is the specification: it states what the ISA is. The hardware has not caught up yet, so the generated ISA Reference (derived from the mininpu/isa SSOT) reports the as-built ISA_VERSION 0.4.0 opcode set. That lag is deliberate and tracked, not a contradiction: read this page for the spec, that page for what today's RTL and ISS actually execute.

docs.title: Table of contents
docs.text:
1. Overview & Invariants
  2. State & Geometry
  3. Instruction Encoding
  4. Flags & Locked Decisions
  5. Timing & Reservation Contract
  6. Compiler Scheduling Contract
  7. Worked Example: 32x32 Tiled Matmul + vpu.lut Epilogue
  8. Deferred / Notes

docs.title: 01 Overview and Invariants
docs.text:
A small, statically-scheduled instruction set of 15 opcodes for a matmul/vector accelerator tile.

  • Issue: in-order, single issue, 1 instruction/cycle.
  • NO producer-busy scoreboard, NO hardware interlock. The hardware never stalls a consumer for an in-flight producer and never detects a clobbered in-flight operand.
  • The compiler owns all hazard avoidance, latency padding, and structural serialization (see Section 6).
  • flush.slot is the only mid-program runtime synchronization primitive; halt is the terminal drain (end of kernel), not a mid-program sync.
  • Nonblocking set = {mxu.matmul, mxu.load.w, mxu.acc.init, async-DMA}; every other op is blocking.
  • Instruction word: 64-bit fixed width; operand storage is fixed per opcode, no runtime type tag.
  • dtype convention: bf16 in SPAD and DRAM (devmem); fp32 in VREG and ACC.
  • Launch: a kernel is a binary of NPU assembly; the command processor loads it into IRAM[0] and starts the sequencer, which runs to the terminal halt.

docs.title: 02 Architectural State and Geometry
docs.text:
Four explicitly-addressed data spaces (analogous to CUDA .global / .shared / .reg plus a tensor accumulator), plus MXU weight buffers and control state. No unified addressing: the space is implied by the opcode/operand slot.

Diagram deferred to the presentation refresh; see the text below for the current model.

space role addressed by geometry devmem off-tile DRAM (host-shared, global), bf16 byte address via literal-pool index + launch patch 256b words (DM_ADDR_WIDTH=27) SPAD on-chip scratchpad, bf16 (explicitly managed, NOT a cache) SPAD row index (10-bit), separate 0-based space 1024 x 256b = 32 KiB, 8 banks (addr % 8) VREG vector register file, fp32 VREG id (5-bit, 0..31) 32 flat 1D fp32 vector registers (2r1w); as-built RTL still 2D [8x8] - see §4 ACC matmul accumulator, fp32 (MXU-owned) ACC buffer index; buffers {0,1} both used (depth-2 double-buffer) [8x8] fp32 x depth 2 = 512 B, 8 per-column banks wbuf weight buffer (MXU, stationary) implicit; mxu.load.w fills inactive, mxu.matmul promotes 2 (double buffer) control PC + induction vars - PC 12-bit; iv_reg x4 (LIFO nest depth 4), 32-bit each LPM literal pool: on-chip constant / param RAM in the tile sequencer (baked + launch-patched, read-only to the datapath) pool-index (8-bit, 0..255) 256 x 64b = ~2 KiB (provisional; final sizing during implementation); NOT devmem, NOT SPAD

docs.title: Datapath spine
docs.text:
stage detail outgoing edge devmem off-tile DRAM, bf16 DMU: async DRAM to SPAD copy. SPAD on-chip scratchpad, bf16 dtype boundary: vreg.load widens bf16 to fp32; vreg.store truncates fp32 to bf16. VREG register file, fp32 MXU burst-reads X (cast fp32 to bf16 at the VREG read port); output lands in ACC. ACC accumulator, fp32 acc.store drains ACC to VREG, exact lossless fp32.
  • W path: SPAD to wbuf via mxu.load.w (weights stationary).
  • X path: VREG to the bf16 x_matrix staging buffer (cast fp32 to bf16 at the VREG read port), then streamed from x_matrix to the PE array.
  • Output path: PE array to ACC.
  • dtype boundary sits only at the SPAD-VREG edge.

docs.title: 2.1 Literal pool (LPM)
docs.text:
A fifth on-chip element, beside the four data spaces plus wbuf and control. It is the tile's constant store.

  • Address space: a separate pool-index space, 8-bit index (0..255); 256 x 64b entries (~2 KiB), provisional (final sizing during implementation); on-chip RAM inside the tile sequencer. NOT devmem, NOT SPAD.
  • Structure: an array of 64-bit constant entries. Entry classes: (a) loop descriptor = {hi bound, packed bo-stride vector}; (b) devmem base address (launch-patched); (c) scalar immediate.
  • Access: read-only to the datapath; read by the sequencer in Decode. Written once per launch = compiler-baked constants + command-processor launch patch (devmem bases via program_id/GRID_LINEAR + kernel_args). Part of the kernel image (loaded with the code).
  • Operands that reference it: loop.begin.op1 (pool index of the loop descriptor {hi, bo-stride vector}), dmu.*.op1 (devmem base; stride at pool[idx+1]), vpu.simd.op2 (immediate when bcast=imm).

docs.title: 2.2 Addressing model
docs.text:
• Effective address of a memory/register operand = operand_literal + bo[channel], where bo is a per-channel base-offset accumulator maintained by the sequencer. There is no address multiplier in the datapath: the per-unit AGU is just an adder.
  • bo is maintained by stride-accumulation over the loop nest: loop.begin pushes the current bo (LIFO) and loads the loop's stride vector from the pool into the loop frame; each loop.end iteration does bo[ch] += stride[ch]; loop exit pops.
  • So bo[ch] = sum over active loops of (iter * stride). The induction variable is the loop counter; the address math is the incremental accumulation (equivalent to const + sum(iv*stride) but multiplier-free).

docs.title: 03 Instruction Encoding
docs.text:
Single 64-bit word, fixed field layout.

 63        56 55      48 47        32 31        16 15         0
+-----------+----------+------------+------------+------------+
|  OP[7:0]  | FLAGS[7:0]|  op0[15:0] |  op1[15:0] |  op2[15:0] |
+-----------+----------+------------+------------+------------+
  • OP[63:56] - 8-bit opcode.
  • FLAGS[55:48] - 8-bit flags byte (per-op; unused bits reserved-must-be-zero).
  • op0[47:32] / op1[31:16] / op2[15:0] - three 16-bit operand slots, storage fixed per opcode.

docs.title: 3.1 Opcode table (SSOT)
docs.text: opcode mnemonic op0 op1 op2 flags writeback 0x00 mxu.load.w SPAD row (W tile) - - [0]=transB (reverse weight-row load order) wbuf (inactive) 0x01 mxu.matmul ACC (acc_out) VREG id (X, streamed) - [0]=accum, [1]=causal ACC 0x02 mxu.acc.init ACC (acc_out) SPAD row (bias, 1xN) - - ACC (bias broadcast to all rows) 0x10 vpu.reduce VREG dst VREG src - [0]=0 sum / 1 max VREG (per-row scalar to col0) 0x11 vpu.lut VREG dst VREG src - [1:0]=0 exp2 / 1 rsqrt / 2 recip / 3 tanh VREG 0x12 vpu.simd VREG dst VREG a VREG b (or pool-imm if bcast=imm) [4:2]=funct (3-bit, 7 defined: 0 add, 1 sub, 2 mul, 3 max, 4 fma, 5 mov, 6 div; 7 reserved), [1:0]=bcast{none,row,col,imm} VREG 0x30 acc.store VREG dst ACC src - - VREG (fp32 lossless) 0x32 vreg.load VREG dst SPAD row (src) - - VREG (bf16 to fp32 widen) 0x33 vreg.store SPAD row (dst) VREG src - - SPAD (fp32 to bf16 truncate) 0x40 dmu.load.spm SPAD row (dst) devmem addr (literal-pool index; stride at pool[idx+1]) shape = (rows-1)<<8 \| (cols-1) [4]=IMM_OP1 SPAD region 0x42 dmu.store.spm SPAD row (src) devmem addr (pool index) shape (0xFFFF = derive at lowering) [4]=IMM_OP1 DRAM 0x50 loop.begin iv id loop descriptor (pool index): (lo<<8) \| step, both 8-bit literals [4]=IMM_OP1 (immediate hi; no-bo loops only) push loop frame, IV=lo 0x51 loop.end iv id - - - IV += step; branch to body if IV < hi 0x53 flush.slot slot bitmask (16-bit immediate) - - - none (async-DMA region fence) 0x5F halt - - - - none (drain all DMU, then assert done)

docs.title: 3.2 Per-op semantics
docs.text:
• mxu.load.w - load W tile from SPAD[op0] into the inactive wbuf; transB reverses row load order; each mxu.matmul consumes exactly one preceding mxu.load.w.

  • mxu.matmul - streaming matmul into ACC[op0] from X = VREG[op1] and the active stationary weights (X cast fp32 to bf16 at the VREG read port):

    acc_out = (accum ? acc_out : 0) + X @ W^T

    causal masks the output upper triangle. A matmul with no pending mxu.load.w is illegal. - mxu.acc.init - seed ACC[op0] with a 1xN bias from SPAD[op1], broadcast to all rows. - vpu.reduce - per-row reduce of VREG[op1] to VREG[op0] col0; flag selects sum / max. - vpu.lut - elementwise unary exp2 / rsqrt / recip / tanh, VREG to VREG. The function set is closed at these four (#385 §5 #7, #381 row 5). gelu is not a LUT function: it is decomposed by the compiler into a primitive sequence over vpu.lut tanh + vpu.simd, with no dedicated vpu.lut slot and no dedicated HW. As-built, 0.4.0 still ships the fused vpu.gelu (0x1D) backed by vpu_gelu_unit.sv; retiring that unit and emitting the decomposition is rework B work. - vpu.simd - elementwise ALU with broadcast mode none/row/col/imm. funct is flags[4:2], 3 bits / 8 slots, 7 defined: 0 add, 1 sub, 2 mul, 3 max, 4 fma, 5 mov, 6 div (slot 7 reserved-must-be-zero). div is #381 AMENDMENT 2, confirmed by the owner in #385 §5 #64: the 8-row div.bcast arm already exists in RTL and GPT-2 softmax uses it, so div keeps a slot rather than lowering to recip + mul. Not every (funct, bcast, shape) combination has an RTL arm; combinations with no arm are not encodable and raise illegal_op (arm inventory: #381). - acc.store - drain ACC[op1] to VREG[op0], exact lossless fp32 (no conversion). - vreg.load - SPAD[op1] to VREG[op0], widen bf16 to fp32. - vreg.store - VREG[op1] to SPAD[op0], truncate fp32 to bf16. - dmu.load.spm - async DRAM to SPAD; async, 2 slots (DMA_SLOT_COUNT=2); region unsafe to consume until a matching flush.slot. - dmu.store.spm - async SPAD to DRAM; op2=0xFFFF derives shape at lowering. - loop.begin - push a loop frame on op0, set IV=lo (LIFO, nest depth 4). op1 is the pool index of the loop descriptor {hi bound, bo-stride vector} (always resident in the literal pool, so the stride vector is always available); the IMM_OP1 flag instead supplies an immediate hi and is used only for loops that drive no bo strides. - loop.end - IV += step; branch to body while IV < hi, else pop. - flush.slot - block until the masked DMU slot(s) report done; the only mid-program runtime sync (halt is the terminal drain, not a mid-program sync). - halt - drain all outstanding DMU, then assert done; ends the kernel.

docs.title: 04 Flags and Locked Decisions
docs.text:
Locked

flags[5] (ACC_TILE) is dropped from vpu.simd; the VPU is VREG-only. Bit 5 is reserved-must-be-zero on all opcodes.

Locked

ACC buffers {0,1} are both used for depth-2 double-buffering (acc_out / acc_src in [0, ACC_DEPTH=2)). The existing ACC buffer-index operand field is now plumbed to select the buffer (the depth index; no encoding change); an index >= ACC_DEPTH is still an illegal-op. This depth-2 buffer index is distinct from the 8 per-column banks (the physical column organization of the [8x8] accumulator).

Design target · rework B (shipped RTL still 2D)

VREG is a flat 1D fp32 vector register file (RISC-V-style; 5-bit id unchanged). The earlier "2D [8x8] tile" lock is withdrawn: its load-bearing premise (that the MXU streams tiles directly from VRegs) is false for this design - the MXU bursts X into its own x_matrix staging buffer, so a standard flat vector RF is the cleaner model. Owner ruling: #385 §5 #1, rationale in #381 Decision B.

As-built: the shipped RTL (vreg_rf.sv, mxu.sv) is still 2D; the flat-1D rework lands in rework B (vreg_rf, addressing, MXU S_LOAD_X, LSU, operand encoding). mininpu/hwparams.py (VREG_SIZE = N) is already 1D. Span across multiple registers is expressed by register grouping (LMUL-style), not by a shape flag; the encoding for that is open and deferred to B's design.

Locked

DMU is async-only: the async-mode flag is retired; DMU flags[5] is reserved-must-be-zero.

docs.title: Reserved-must-be-zero
docs.text:
• All unused flag bits on every opcode.
  • flags[5] on all opcodes (dropped ACC_TILE).
  • DMU flags[5] (retired async-mode flag).

docs.title: 05 Timing and Reservation Contract
docs.text: Because there is no hardware interlock or scoreboard, the per-instruction cycle-accurate access windows are a normative compiler-hardware contract.

docs.title: 5.0 Notation
docs.text:
Single definition point for every symbol used across this document. Kind distinguishes ISA-visible structural constants from RTL / synth-measured implementation values (not locked) from reference/index symbols.

symbol meaning kind value / status t issue cycle of an instruction (reference point for all access windows) reference - N systolic tile dimension (rows/cols of the array; also the whole-tile-row width) STRUCTURAL, ISA-visible 8 (baseline) 2N-1 systolic array fill/drain span STRUCTURAL 15 at N=8 d matmul issue-to-first-X-read offset: matmul reads VREG[X] over [t+d, t+d+N) RTL / synth-measured TBD L_matmul matmul latency: issue to ACC written (ACC valid at t+L_matmul) RTL / synth-measured TBD R_mxu the MXU's VREG read PORT used to stream X (a port name, not a latency) port id - L_ldw mxu.load.w latency: issue to inactive wbuf loaded RTL / synth-measured ~N D_vpu VPU pipeline depth = per-row drain latency (result at t+D_vpu); row-pipelined for the single-source SIMD ops (1 tile-row/cycle), gelu / exp2 / two-source latency-serial (1 row / D_vpu) RTL / synth-measured, NOT a locked constant about 23 (measured after the fp-pipeline pass) PE_LATENCY (MXU PE pipeline depth) registered-MAC stages inside each PE (adds to the 2N-1 fill) RTL / synth-measured 2 (measured); matmul fill = 2N-1 + PE_LATENCY = 17 lo / hi / step loop lower bound / upper bound / increment (lo, step 8-bit immediate; hi from the literal-pool descriptor) index - iv loop induction variable (per active loop frame) index - bo[channel] per-channel base-offset accumulator; eff_addr = operand_literal + bo[channel] state 8 channels m, n, k output-row-tile / output-col-tile / contraction tile indices in the 4x4x4 grid index 0..3
  • The contract lives in the ISA but is implementation-versioned: the structure is fixed now; the absolute offsets are TBD (RTL-measured) and locked by the sim == synth requirement.
  • A kernel binary declares the timing-rev it was compiled against; a hardware timing-rev mismatch is rejected / flagged. This is the concrete form of "recompile if latencies change".
  • The concrete structural values are N and the systolic fill 2N-1. The VPU pipeline depth D_vpu, the MXU PE pipeline depth, the offsets (d, L_matmul), and all absolute numbers are RTL / synth-timing measured implementation values - D_vpu is currently about 23 (measured after the fp-pipeline pass), NOT an ISA-visible structural constant.
  • Throughput-bound, not latency-bound: these per-instruction latencies (including D_vpu and the matmul systolic fill) are negligible for performance - the tile is throughput-bound, so pipeline depth may grow to meet 200 MHz while throughput stays 1 tile-row/cycle (VPU) and 1 column/cycle (MXU). The VPU is row-pipelined for the single-source SIMD ops (scale.bcast, scale.col.bcast, scale.imm, sub.bcast, add.col.bcast): 1 tile-row issued per cycle, multiple rows in flight, and D_vpu is the per-row drain latency, amortized across the tile-loop. Row-pipelining is partial: gelu / exp2 and the two-source ops stay latency-serial (each row drains the full D_vpu before the next issues). The deep fp / reduce-tree pipelining (combinational fp_mul, registered IEEE fp_add) is intended design, not overhead.

Structural access windows (offsets symbolic; issue cycle = t):

instruction reads (port, window) writes (port, @cycle) notes mxu.matmul VREG[X] on R_mxu over [t+d, t+d+N) (1 tile-row/cycle); wbuf(active) implicitly ACC at t + L_matmul the PE MAC is registered for 200 MHz with PE_LATENCY = 2 (measured), so fill = 2N-1 + PE_LATENCY = 17; VREG[X] is WAR-free only after t+d+N. The 1 tile-row/cycle above is the VREG X-read burst rate (N cycles, into x_matrix), a different quantity from the array's steady-state 1 column/cycle throughput mxu.load.w SPAD over ~N cycles into wbuf(inactive) wbuf(inactive) double-buffered, so it overlaps the concurrent matmul streaming the other buffer mxu.acc.init SPAD bias row ACC (broadcast to all rows) - vpu.reduce / vpu.lut / vpu.simd VREG (x2 reads for simd, on the two whole-tile-row read ports) VREG on the single write port at t + D_vpu D_vpu = VPU pipeline depth, RTL / synth-measured (as-built 0.4.0: 23, measured after the fp-pipeline pass); an implementation number, not a locked constant. Fixed and uniform across every function, so a result is available at issue + D_vpu. The as-built depth-setter is the fused gelu, which 0.5.0 retires, so D_vpu may shrink at the cutover (microarch) acc.store ACC VREG on the single write port (LSU-driven) exact lossless fp32 vreg.load / vreg.store LSU: SPAD <-> VREG LSU: SPAD <-> VREG bf16 <-> fp32 cast at the boundary

VREG is 2r1w: the VPU writeback and the LSU writeback (acc.store / vreg.load) name the same single physical write port, time-shared by the compiler schedule so two writes never collide in a cycle. A 2nd write port is a sizing option only, not part of the baseline.

TBD · offsets RTL / synth-timing measured

The MXU PE pipeline depth is PE_LATENCY = 2 (measured), so the matmul fill = 2N-1 + PE_LATENCY = 17. d, L_matmul, the VPU pipeline depth D_vpu (currently about 23, measured after the fp-pipeline pass), and the remaining absolute numbers are TBD (RTL / synth-timing measured). Only N and 2N-1 are the ISA-visible concrete structural values. All latencies are FIXED and data-independent once measured, since the no-scoreboard model relies on it: simulation latency must equal synthesis latency - a wrong locked latency (a value that disagrees with the RTL-measured one) silently miscompiles.

docs.title: 06 Compiler Scheduling Contract
docs.text:
With no interlock and no scoreboard, program correctness depends entirely on the static schedule. The compiler MUST obey every rule.

  • RAW (latency-respecting placement): schedule a consumer no earlier than producer-issue + producer-latency; fill gaps with independent ops or NOPs.
  • WAR on inputs is unguarded: never overwrite a VREG / SPAD row / ACC buffer still being read by an in-flight instruction.
  • WAW ordering: two writes to the same destination must be program-ordered with non-overlapping write windows.
  • Structural hazards: one matmul at a time; exactly one mxu.load.w per matmul (single inactive wbuf); one VPU op-stream at a time.
  • VREG 2r1w (whole-tile-row): at most 2 reads + 1 write per cycle; keep the foreground unit's (VPU / LSU) VREG accesses in cycles disjoint from the MXU X-read burst (temporal separation, not banking).
  • DMU ordering: insert flush.slot before consuming a dmu.load.spm region; ensure producing vreg.stores complete before a dmu.store.spm reads; fence two async DMAs to the same region.
  • ACC discipline: seed (mxu.acc.init or accum=0), accumulate (accum=1, same buffer, in order), drain (acc.store, LSU-driven).
  • Loops: literal-only trip counts (lo/step 8-bit, hi from the pool); nest depth 4 (LIFO); no runtime-data-derived counts.
  • Addressing: compile-baked (literals + launch-patched pool indices + IV-relative base + iv*stride); no runtime data-dependent addressing in v1.
  • Recompilation: a binary is valid only for the latencies it was compiled against; recompile if any FU latency, N, or D_vpu changes.

docs.title: 07 Worked Example: 32x32 Tiled Matmul + Bias + vpu.lut Epilogue
docs.text:
A full tile-loop example: f(X @ W^T + b), where f is any elementwise vpu.lut function (the example issues exp2; the activation is incidental to what this section teaches). Assume X already in VREG, and W and bias already in SPAD (DMU excluded). Geometry N=8, so a 32x32 matmul is a 4x4x4 tile grid (m,n,k in 0..3).

  • Layout: X[m][k] at VREG id 4m+k; Y scratch VREG16; W row = 16n+4k; bias row = n; Y row = 16m+4n.

docs.title: 7.0 Initial architectural state
docs.text:
What lives where before the tile-loop runs. Note the dtype boundary: bf16 in SPAD, fp32 in VREG and ACC (the cast sits at the SPAD-VREG edge).

space dtype / geometry contents before the loop VREG fp32, as-built 32 x [8x8] X[m][k] tile at id 4m+k (ids 0..15); Y scratch at V16 - the compute working set SPAD bf16 W^T rows at row 16n+4k; bias rows at row n (base B_base) - the matmul operands LPM 64b entries the loop descriptors pool[0..2] + any pooled bases (see 7.0b) ACC fp32, MXU-owned, depth-2 empty; seeded per (m,n) block by acc.init, drained by acc.store

docs.title: 7.0b Literal pool contents
docs.text:
The concrete pool entries this kernel needs, so loop.begin op1 = pool[idx] (referenced in 7.2 as pool[2]={hi=4, k bo-strides}) is grounded. Stride values are taken from the 7.1 loop.end comments and match the 7.3 bo trace.

entry class contents pool[0] m-loop descriptor {hi=4; bo-stride: bo_X+=4, bo_Ys+=16} pool[1] n-loop descriptor {hi=4; bo_W+=16, bo_B+=1, bo_Ys+=4} pool[2] k-loop descriptor {hi=4; bo_X+=1, bo_W+=4} pool[3..] pooled bases (launch-patched) B_base, W_base, X_base, Y_base; the devmem base is patched at launch

docs.title: 7.1 Static program (body once, 12 static instructions)
docs.text:
loop.begin m (iv0,0,4,1)          ; push bo
  loop.begin n (iv1,0,4,1)        ; push bo
    acc.init  ACC0, B_base         ; bias row = B_base + bo_B
    loop.begin k (iv2,0,4,1)      ; push bo
      load.w  W_base               ; W row   = W_base + bo_W
      matmul  ACC0, X_base, accum  ; X id    = X_base + bo_X
    loop.end  k                    ; iter: bo_X+=1, bo_W+=4  | exit: pop
    acc.store V16, ACC0
    vpu.lut   V16, V16, exp2       ; fn=0; see the gelu note below
    vreg.store Y_base, V16         ; Y row   = Y_base + bo_Ys
  loop.end  n                      ; iter: bo_W+=16, bo_B+=1, bo_Ys+=4 | exit: pop
loop.end  m                        ; iter: bo_X+=4, bo_Ys+=16 | exit: pop

Why exp2 and not gelu (owner ruling #385 §5 #7)

This example previously issued vpu.lut V16, V16, gelu with FLAGS = 02. Under the ruled function set {0 exp2, 1 rsqrt, 2 recip, 3 tanh} there is no gelu slot, and 0x02 now decodes to recip - so the old example was doubly wrong. It is rewritten to exp2 (FLAGS = 00), which has a tile-shaped RTL arm and is unambiguous. The choice of activation is incidental to what §7 demonstrates (the loop / base-offset / double-buffer structure).

Why the swap is timing-neutral: exp2 and gelu are the same row shape - both dispatch to VPU_ACC_ROW and run latency-serial, one tile-row per D_vpu (vpu_ctrl.sv:56), so the §7.2-§7.4 tail numbers are unchanged. The shared uniform terminal alone is not the reason, and this argument does not generalize to every vpu.lut function: rsqrt dispatches to VPU_VREG_EXEC (vpu_ctrl.sv:63) and runs parallel across all 8 rows in ~24 cycles rather than 8 x 24, an 8x row-shape difference even though its issue-to-write terminal is the identical D_vpu. Substituting rsqrt here would change the tail.

The gelu decomposition sequence is deliberately not written here, and writing it is blocked on two missing arms. gelu_new needs a tile-shaped tanh - as-built 0.4.0 has no ISA-visible tanh op at all (fp_tanh_bram exists only as a per-lane 1-clk primitive wired inside vpu_gelu_unit.sv:120, reachable only via the fused vpu.gelu; the as-built TANH(43) arm is 1-row only) - and a tile-shaped fma/add with an immediate: the one multiply-add arm that does exist, OP_VPU_FMA_IMM = 0x27, is VREG-only (VPU_VREG_EXEC), not the tile-shaped ACC-row arm a decomposed gelu would need. The shape axis (flags[5]) is itself open pending rework B's flat-1D VREG design. Deriving the sequence is rework B work, tracked in #381; inventing one here would freeze a convention that has not been decided.

docs.title: 7.2 64-bit encoding (example block m=1, n=2)
docs.text:
Word = OP[63:56] | FLAGS[55:48] | op0[47:32] | op1[31:16] | op2[15:0].

# asm OP FLAGS op0 op1 op2 effective addr 1 acc.init ACC0,B(n) 02 00 0000 0200 0000 bias = 0x200 + bo_B(2) = 0x202 2 loop.begin k 50 00 0000 0002 0001 op1=pool[2]={hi=4, k bo-strides}; op2 lo<<8\|step=0x0001 3 load.w W 00 00 0000 0000 0000 W row = 0 + bo_W(0x20) + k*4 4 matmul ACC0,X,accum 01 01 0000 0000 0000 X id = 0 + bo_X(4) + k = 4..7 5 loop.end k 51 00 0000 0000 0000 iv+=1, branch if k<4 6 acc.store V16,ACC0 30 00 0010 0000 0000 - 7 vpu.lut V16,V16,exp2 11 00 0010 0010 0000 FLAGS [1:0]=0 -> fn=exp2 8 vreg.store Y,V16 33 00 0300 0010 0000 Y row = 0x300 + bo_Ys(0x18) = 0x318

docs.title: 7.4 Cycle-level Gantt for one (m,n) block
docs.text:
Structural (N=8); the absolute L_* are implementation-versioned - FIG. 9 below uses the current RTL/synth-measured example latencies.

cy ->  0    2    4    6    8   10   12   14   16 ...(N=8 units)
SEQ  : Iai  Ilb  Ilw0 [nop/indep to cover L_ldw] Imm0 Ilw1 Imm1 Ilw2 Imm2 Ilw3 Imm3 Ile ... Ias Ilut Ivs
wbufA:      [ load W0 =N cy ][ stream mm0 =N cy ][ load W2 ][ stream mm2 ]
wbufB:                       [ load W1 =N cy  ][ stream mm1 ][ load W3 ][ stream mm3 ]
ACC  : [init]              +=mm0  +=mm1  +=mm2  +=mm3 -> drain(2N-1) -> acc.store -> lut -> vreg.store

FIG. 9 - tile-loop pipeline Gantt for one (m,n) block: SEQ issue stream, weight double-buffer ping-pong (load.w ~ N=8 fully hidden), ACC init to += mm0..mm3 (each after fill = 2N-1 + PE_LATENCY = 17), then the LSU / VPU tail (acc.store to vpu.lut exp2 to vreg.store), with the ACC depth-2 overlap. The tail op matches §7.1: vpu.lut exp2, latency-serial at one tile-row per D_vpu (as-built D_vpu = 23, so 8 rows occupy 8 x 24 = 192 cycles - occupancy is latency + 1 reset cycle per row). open standalone

  • Steady state = 1 matmul / N=8 cy, load.w fully overlapped in the other weight buffer; absolute block time TBD; ACC depth-2 lets block j tail overlap block j+1 matmul.

docs.title: 08 Deferred / Notes
docs.text:
Status · 0.5.0 is the frozen spec; SSOT cutover pending

ISA 0.5.0 is the frozen spec (amendments tracked in #381). The shipped SSOT (mininpu/isa/opcodes.py) is still as-built 0.4.0; the cutover to the 0.5.0 opcode set (incl. acc.init, vpu.lut function-select, vreg.store) plus encoders.py / ISS / npu_isa_pkg.sv regeneration and PE_LATENCY 0 -> 2 is PENDING (tracked separately; affects the compiler).

Per-opcode counts for the as-built 0.4.0 set are not restated here: the generated, drift-guarded ISA Reference derives them from mininpu/isa at build time and is gated by dv/test_abi_contract.py::test_generated_docs_up_to_date.

  • ACC buffer-index operand: now plumbed for depth-2 double-buffering (buffers {0,1}, the depth index, not the 8 per-column banks; no encoding change); a deeper ACC depth sweep (> 2) remains deferred (#313 / #262).
  • MXU addressing: drive path truncates to 13-bit (interim).
  • N: N=8 baseline; N is the systolic tile dimension, ISA-visible but sweepable without an encoding change (N=16 is an ISA-invariant sweep target: the same N realized as a wider 16x16 array, not an independent outer tiling factor).

End of mininpu Compute-Tile ISA Reference v0.5.0.

mininpu · ISA 0.5.0 (Doc 1, locked) · back to index

docs.title: Reference Microarchitecture
docs.text:
mininpu · Doc 2 of 3 · In progress

The datapath specified against ISA 0.5.0 (as-built RTL is 0.4.0; cutover pending): fully-exposed 4-stage pipeline, per-unit AGU, MXU / VPU / VREG / ACC / SPAD, and the compiler-timed overlap model. Locked design plus the still-open decisions.

  • 1 instr/cycle, no scoreboard
  • pipeline F D X W
  • MXU 8x8 systolic
  • VPU 8 lanes
  • VREG 2r1w tiles

docs.title: Table of contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Execution model / pipeline
  3. Functional units & memories
  4. Overlap & roofline
  5. Decisions pending

This document describes the compute-tile microarchitecture specified against ISA 0.5.0. Content is split into a Locked design and a Decisions pending section (still-open items).

Spec vs RTL as-built

Spec: ISA 0.5.0 (frozen; amendments tracked in #381) · RTL as-built: 0.4.0 · cutover pending. This page specifies the datapath against 0.5.0; the RTL on main does not implement it yet (the cutover also carries PE_LATENCY 0 -> 2). See the status note, and the generated ISA Reference for the as-built set.

docs.title: 01 Overview
docs.text:
Top-level compute tile: the devmem-SPAD-VREG-ACC spine plus the MXU, VPU, and tile sequencer.

FIG. 1-2 - compute-tile datapath overview: the devmem to SPAD to VREG to ACC spine plus the MXU, VPU, and tile sequencer. open standalone

docs.title: Datapath spine
docs.text:
• devmem: off-tile DRAM (global)
    • DMU: async DRAM to SPAD.
  • SPAD: on-chip scratchpad, bf16
    • cast on vreg.load / vreg.store (bf16 to/from fp32).
  • VREG: vector register file, fp32
    • MXU burst-reads X (cast fp32 to bf16 at the VREG read port, into the bf16 x_matrix staging buffer); output to ACC.
  • ACC: matmul accumulator, fp32
    • acc.store drains ACC back to VREG (lossless fp32).

docs.title: Concurrency model
docs.text:
In-order issue, 1 instruction/cycle. No producer-busy scoreboard; no hardware interlock. All hazard avoidance is static compiler scheduling. flush.slot is the only mid-program runtime synchronization (halt is the terminal drain at end of kernel).

Nonblocking set = {mxu.matmul, mxu.load.w, mxu.acc.init, async-DMA}; everything else is blocking.

docs.title: Distributed per-unit AGU
docs.text:
Each unit has its own address-generation unit. The AGU is an adder: effective address = operand_literal + bo[channel], no multiplier.

operand_literal is a compile-baked immediate or pool index (devmem bases GRID_LINEAR launch-patched, not a CSR); bo is the per-channel base-offset broadcast from the tile sequencer.

docs.title: Base-offset (bo) addressing
docs.text:
8 base-offset channels in the sequencer; the AGU adds literal + bo with no multiplier.

bo is maintained by pool-sourced stride vectors: LIFO push/pop at loop.begin / loop exit, and bo[ch] += stride[ch] at each loop.end. Full rule: the ISA addressing model.

docs.title: Literal pool (LPM)
docs.text:
On-chip param RAM in the sequencer (256 x 64b (~2 KiB), provisional; final sizing during implementation), pool-index space, baked + launch-patched, read in Decode.

Realization: reuse the mature IRAM-embedded literal-pool machinery (loaded with the kernel image, read in Decode via the redirect mux), not a new separate RAM (see the implementation note).

Holds loop descriptors (hi + stride vector), devmem bases, and scalar immediates. See the ISA literal-pool section.

docs.title: 02 Execution model / pipeline
docs.text:
Locked - the fully-exposed, compiler-timed pipeline.

Performance model · throughput-bound, not latency-bound

The compute tile is throughput-bound. Per-instruction latency - including D_vpu and the matmul systolic fill - is negligible for performance: it is the pipeline drain latency, amortized across the tile-loop, not on a serial critical path. So pipeline depth may grow freely to meet timing while throughput stays 1 tile-row/cycle (VPU) and 1 column/cycle (MXU).

This rests on row-pipelining, which is partial. The single-source SIMD ops (scale.bcast, scale.col.bcast, scale.imm, sub.bcast, add.col.bcast) stream at 1 tile-row/cycle with multiple rows in flight (rows overlapped); for them D_vpu is only the drain latency of a single row, paid once and hidden behind the following rows, hence negligible. gelu / exp2 and the two-source ops stay latency-serial (each row drains the full D_vpu before the next issues). softmax / LayerNorm are dependent reduction chains within a row, but the tile-loop supplies the row-level parallelism the pipeline consumes, so for the streamable ops the pipe stays full.

The level-wise reduce-tree pipelining and the deep fp pipelines are intended design, not overhead: fp arithmetic follows standard pipelined designs (FloPoCo-style; fp_mul combinational, IEEE fp_add registered / multi-stage), so deepening a stage to close 200 MHz spends latency (which does not matter) to preserve throughput (which does).

  • F: fetch
    • fetch IRAM[PC], 1/cy.
  • D: decode
    • decode + per-unit AGU address gen + iv broadcast.
  • X: execute
    • op-specific latency.

  • W: writeback

    • to VREG / ACC / SPAD / DRAM per the op's writeback target.

  • Stages: F (fetch IRAM[PC], 1/cy) to D (decode + per-unit AGU address gen + iv broadcast) to X (execute, op-specific latency) to W (writeback to VREG / ACC / SPAD / DRAM per the op's writeback target). This is the architectural issue model; the physical Fetch is a pipelined multi-cycle IRAM BRAM read (~3 cycles) that still sustains 1 instruction/cycle issue in steady state (see the Doc 3 tile-sequencer FSM).

  • Fully exposed pipeline: the sequencer issues exactly 1 instruction per cycle and NEVER stalls to wait on a result. The only runtime waits are flush.slot (the sole mid-program sync, waits on an async-DMA slot) and halt (the terminal drain at end of kernel, not a mid-program sync).
  • Fixed, data-independent latencies: the compiler covers every latency by placing dependents later and filling gaps with independent instructions or NOPs. No hardware interlock, no scoreboard.
  • Nonblocking ops (mxu.matmul, mxu.load.w, mxu.acc.init, async-DMA) run in background FUs: the sequencer issues them in 1 cycle and proceeds. The compiler overlaps them (e.g. a background matmul concurrent with a foreground VPU op) and double-buffers the shared VREG tiles.
  • Completion: compute nonblocking ops are fixed-latency, so they need NO fence (compiler-timed). async-DMA is variable-latency, so the compiler inserts flush.slot before any consumer of the DMA'd region.
  • Structural single-instance limits: 1 MXU (matmuls serialize), 1 inactive weight buffer (at most 1 load.w preload ahead), 2 outstanding DMA slots (DMA_SLOT_COUNT=2; flush.slot's bitmask selects among them, async-only).
  • Latencies: the structurally-fixed values are N and the systolic fill = 2N-1; the VPU pipeline depth D_vpu, the registered-PE MAC / PE pipeline depth, and the absolute offsets (d, L_matmul) are RTL / synth-timing measured implementation numbers (D_vpu currently about 23 (measured after the fp-pipeline pass)), NOT ISA-visible structural constants.

FIG. 6 - tile sequencer: IRAM + folded PC/decoder, iv broadcast, loop-frame stack. open standalone

docs.title: 03 Functional units and memories
docs.text: Locked - unit geometry and roles.

docs.title: MXU
docs.text:
8x8 weight-stationary systolic array, bf16 x bf16 to fp32 accumulate, 2 weight buffers (active / inactive double-buffer).

X is burst-read from VREG (N tile-rows, 1 tile-row/cycle), cast fp32 to bf16 at the VREG read port, into the MXU's bf16 x_matrix staging buffer; the array then streams X from x_matrix and does not touch VREG for the rest of the matmul. Output to ACC. Weight-stationary dataflow.

PE MAC is registered for 200 MHz (PE latency to be confirmed by synth); systolic fill = 2N-1 plus the PE pipeline depth.

docs.title: VPU
docs.text:
Fixed-depth pipeline, 8 lanes, function-select {reduce | lut | simd}. Lane = column, row-serial: one 256-bit tile-row per cycle, 8 columns in parallel (the natural structure for the per-row reductions in softmax / LayerNorm). Throughput = 1 tile-row/cycle (row-pipelined, multiple rows in flight) for the single-source SIMD ops; gelu / exp2 / two-source ops are latency-serial (1 row / D_vpu).

Pipeline depth D_vpu is RTL / synth-measured (currently about 23 (measured after the fp-pipeline pass)), an implementation number, NOT an ISA-visible structural constant. fp_mul stays combinational (maps to a DSP), but an IEEE fp_add is registered (1+ pipeline stages, standard FloPoCo-style: a single combinational IEEE fp_add is ~11 ns and misses the 5 ns / 200 MHz budget); the reduce-sum tree is level-wise pipelined and each BRAM-read to compute cone is registered. D_vpu is therefore the sum of registered stages (RTL-measured), not an fp-op count. Whatever its value, it is the uniform issue-to-write terminal for every function (reduce tree, exp2 BRAM, rsqrt, simd all align to it), so the reservation window is single. Reuses the fpu/* primitives (exp2 / rsqrt / tanh / recip BRAM LUTs, fp_mul / fp_add).

Open: which function sets D_vpu under ISA 0.5.0

The measured 23 is pinned to the as-built 0.4.0 datapath, where the depth-setting function is the fused vpu.gelu (OP_VPU_GELU = 0x1D) and every shorter op is padded up to it (vpu_ctrl.sv:417, :436-446). ISA 0.5.0 retires gelu as an opcode (it decomposes), so the uniform terminal would be set by the next-deepest surviving function and may be smaller than 23. The new value is not yet measured and is not asserted here; it is an output of the 0.5.0 cutover plus the pending gelu-decomposition design (see the ISA 0.5.0 status note). Do not treat 23 as a 0.5.0 number.

docs.title: VREG
docs.text:
Design target: 32 flat 1D fp32 vector registers (VREG_SIZE = N; owner ruling #385 §5 #1). As-built: 32 x [8x8] fp32 = 8 KiB with whole-tile-row access (256-bit). 2 read + 1 write ports (2r1w baseline) either way. See the realization subsection.

No banking: MXU / VPU / LSU conflicts are avoided by temporal separation (foreground VREG access scheduled outside the short MXU X-read burst). See the realization subsection.

docs.title: ACC
docs.text:
[8x8] fp32 x depth 2 = 512 B. 8 per-column banks (the physical column organization: one accumulator column per systolic column). Separately, ACC buffers {0,1} are both used for depth-2 double-buffering; the ACC buffer field (the depth index) selects the buffer, not a column bank.

Depth-2 double-buffer: the second buffer overlaps a block's drain/store tail (acc.store → vpu.lut → vreg.store) with the next block's matmul.

docs.title: SPAD
docs.text: bf16 storage. Separate, 0-based on-chip address space (like CUDA .shared). 32 KiB, 8 banks.

docs.title: LSU (load/store unit)
docs.text: A small dedicated load/store unit handling acc.store (ACC to VREG, fp32), vreg.load (SPAD to VREG, bf16 to fp32), and vreg.store (VREG to SPAD, fp32 to bf16).

docs.title: Loop control
docs.text:
loop.end branch bubble = 0: a counted loop with a precomputed target.

Full-unroll is infeasible (the unrolled GPT-2 static instruction count far exceeds the 4096-deep IRAM; a wider N=16 systolic array - the same systolic tile dimension N, a microarch sweep target, not an independent outer factor - gives about 8x fewer tiles than N=8), so outer tiling must be in-kernel nested loops driven by the base-offset (bo) accumulators (full rule: the ISA addressing model).

docs.title: Conflict model
docs.text:
No runtime interlock or scoreboard: the compiler statically avoids all VREG / SPAD port and bank conflicts using the reservation table (see the ISA timing contract); sim == synth is mandatory.

Optional debug-only conflict detector flags same-port / same-bank same-cycle access in debug builds (no stall): an optional bring-up aid, not part of the shipping datapath.

docs.title: Shared fp primitives
docs.text: FIG. 11 - shared fp primitives, stage/latency view: fp_add LATENCY=2 ({align+add} | {normalize} | {pack}, LUT/CARRY8) and fp_mul LATENCY=2 ({unpack} | {DSP48} | {round+pack}). The atoms every unit below is built from. open standalone

docs.title: MXU
docs.text:
FIG. 7 - MXU datapath: 8x8 weight-stationary systolic array, 2 weight buffers, X burst-read from VREG (cast to bf16 at the VREG read port, into the bf16 x_matrix staging buffer), output to ACC. open standalone

FIG. 12 - MXU matmul pipeline, stage/latency view: X burst-read (VREG) to fp32->bf16 cast at the VREG read port to the bf16 x_matrix staging buffer to 8x8 systolic array (SKEW = PE_LATENCY+1 = 3, per-PE MAC = combinational fp_mul + registered fp_add L=2) to mxu_accumulator causal-fold RMW to ACC. Reservation offsets d(r) = 2N + 3r, PE_LAT = 5. Structural figure is FIG. 7, tile-loop Gantt FIG. 9. open standalone

docs.title: VPU pipeline
docs.text:
Locked structure - fixed-depth, partially row-pipelined vector pipeline (single-source SIMD ops stream; gelu / exp2 / two-source stay latency-serial); the depth D_vpu is RTL / synth-measured (currently about 23, measured after the fp-pipeline pass), not a locked constant.

  • Structure: 8 lanes (lane = column), row-serial at one 256-bit tile-row per cycle, throughput 1 tile-row/cycle (row-pipelined: multiple tile-rows in flight, 1 issued per cycle, each draining after D_vpu cycles) for the single-source SIMD ops only (scale.bcast, scale.col.bcast, scale.imm, sub.bcast, add.col.bcast); gelu / exp2 / two-source ops are latency-serial (1 row / D_vpu). fp_mul stays combinational (DSP), but an IEEE fp_add is registered (1+ pipeline stages, standard FloPoCo-style: a single combinational IEEE fp_add is ~11 ns and misses the 5 ns / 200 MHz budget); the reduce-sum tree is level-wise pipelined and each BRAM-read to compute cone is registered.
  • Depth is RTL-measured, set by the deepest function: D_vpu is the sum of the registered stages along the deepest function, measured from RTL / synth, not a hand-counted fp-op total; every other function aligns to the same terminal so the reservation window is single. As-built 0.4.0: the deepest function is the fused vpu.gelu (fp_mul / registered fp_add chain + the tanh BRAM lookup) and D_vpu = 23 (vpu_ctrl.sv:417, measured after the fp-pipeline pass). Under ISA 0.5.0 gelu is retired, so the depth-setter becomes the next-deepest surviving function and D_vpu may drop below 23; that value is unmeasured (see the open note in the VPU section above). D_vpu is an implementation number, in the same bucket as L_matmul and the PE pipeline depth, NOT an ISA-visible structural constant (unlike N and 2N-1).
  • Latency vs occupancy - distinct quantities. D_vpu = 23 is the issue-to-write latency terminal, uniform across every ACC_ROW function (ST_ROW_EXP2 == ST_ROW_GELU == ST_ROW_PLAIN == ST_ROW_TWO == D_VPU, vpu_ctrl.sv:436-446). The per-row FSM occupancy is 24 cycles = latency 23 + 1 reset cycle (stage_cnt runs 0..23 inclusive, then resets on row advance: vpu_ctrl.sv:1153-1161). An 8-row latency-serial op therefore occupies 8 x 24 = 192 cycles, not 8 x 23. Both numbers are correct; do not conflate them.

FIG. 5 - VPU datapath: 8-lane fixed-depth pipeline (lane = column, row-serial), combinational fp_mul + registered IEEE fp_add, level-wise pipelined reduce tree. open standalone

FIG. 10 - VPU row-pipelining: latency-serial (8 x D_vpu = 184 cy) vs row-pipelined (~8 + 23 = 31 cy) schedules for an 8-row tile through the D_vpu = 23 pipeline; D_vpu is the per-row drain, amortized across the tile-loop. open standalone

FIG. 13 - VPU pipeline to the uniform D_vpu = 23 terminal, stage/latency view, as-built 0.4.0: per-function depths within the 23-cycle envelope (the fused gelu, ~22 stages, sets it; exp2 = 5, reduce-tree = 9, single-src SIMD shallow, all padded to 23). The 5 single-source SIMD ops are row-pipelined; gelu / exp2 / two-source ops stay latency-serial. 0.5.0 retires the fused gelu, so the depth-setter changes at the cutover. open standalone

docs.title: SPAD
docs.text: FIG. 8 - SPAD datapath: 32 KiB, 8 banks (bank = row mod 8), bf16; compute clients fixed-latency, the DMU yields on a bank conflict so compute never stalls. open standalone

docs.title: LSU pipeline
docs.text: FIG. 14 - LSU pipeline, stage/latency view: FIXED LATENCY = ROWS + SPAD_RD_LATENCY = 8 + 2 = 10; the three ops (acc.store, vreg.load, vreg.store) share one datapath, read tile-row r on cycle r, write RL=2 later, and own the bf16 ↔ fp32 casts. open standalone

docs.title: VREG realization (whole-tile-row RF + MXU-burst temporal separation)
docs.text:
Resolved model. The 2r1w / temporal-separation half is locked; the register shape is a design target (rework B), not a lock.

  • Physical model (design target): VREG = 32 registers, each a flat 1D fp32 vector (hwparams VREG_SIZE = N, already 1D). The earlier "each register is an [8x8] fp32 tile / VREG_SIZE 8 -> 64" model is withdrawn: its load-bearing premise (that the MXU streams tiles directly from VRegs) is false for this design, since the MXU bursts X into its own x_matrix staging buffer. Owner ruling #385 §5 #1; rationale in #381 Decision B.
  • As-built: the elaborated tile datapath is still 2D - tile_top.sv:396 instantiates vreg_rf (32 x [8x8] fp32, 8 KiB) at a whole tile-row (256-bit) access granularity. (vpu_ctrl.sv:1316 separately instantiates the 1D vec_regfile, 32 x 8 fp32; unifying the VPU onto the shared RF is the DECISION 1 follow-on.) The flat-1D rework lands in rework B.
  • Ports: 2 read + 1 write (whole tile-row), matching the whole-tile-row 2r1w RF. A 2nd write port is a sizing option only if two writes ever need to be concurrent; tail sub-ops are dependency-serial, so 2r1w is the baseline.
  • MXU X read: the MXU reads its X tile from VREG in a short burst (~N cycles) into its x_matrix staging buffer, then does not touch VREG for the rest of the matmul.
  • Conflict avoidance = temporal separation, not banking: the compiler schedules the foreground unit's (VPU / LSU) VREG accesses outside the MXU X-read burst window. Both windows are short, so this is easily schedulable.

Compiler rule

Keep the MXU X-read burst and any concurrent VPU / LSU VREG access in disjoint cycles. The MXU burst is a small fraction of the matmul, so the foreground unit has VREG to itself otherwise. Tail ordering is handled by the scheduler. The VPU has a single fixed pipeline depth (D_vpu, RTL / synth-measured, uniform across functions), so a VPU result is available at issue + D_vpu.

docs.title: MXU: reuse vs change vs the existing RTL
docs.text:
Locked - keep the trusted systolic core, evolve the memory interface toward the VREG tier.

aspect existing RTL paper design plan Systolic core + PE 8x8 weight-stationary array, bf16xbf16->fp32 MAC (pe.sv / systolic.sv) same reuse unchanged Weight source + double buffer SPAD -> 2 weight buffers (active/inactive), WPL concurrent preload same reuse Causal mask -inf fold at drain same reuse trans_b weight-row-reversal load same reuse X source SPAD VREG (read via R_mxu into the X staging buffer) change: re-source X from VREG fp32 -> bf16 cast on the SPAD read path cast at the MXU input stage change: move cast onto the VREG->staging path Matmul result stored back to SPAD stays in ACC change: remove store-back (drop S_STORE) ACC drain (acc.store) - result stays in ACC; the LSU drains ACC -> VREG fp32 change: add the drain, owned by the LSU (not the MXU) Bias init - acc.init = SPAD bias -> ACC broadcast change: add acc.init ACC buffering single depth-2 double-buffer, buffer field {0,1} (the depth index, not the 8 per-column banks) change: plumb the ACC buffer field
  • Reconcile: keep the full-tile X staging buffer (the existing RTL already has one). It shortens the R_mxu VREG occupancy to a ~N-cycle burst, which is what makes the temporal-separation VREG overlap (2r1w) conflict-free.
  • Timing note (corrected): matmul is staged, not a pure N-cycle stream; VREG reads are faster than the old SPAD path; absolute cycles are TBD (RTL, sim == synth). Keep 2N-1 as the weight-march / switch wavefront only.
  • File plan: pe.sv + systolic.sv unchanged; mxu.sv FSM re-sources X from VREG, drops S_STORE, adds the acc.init state + buffer wiring; mxu_accumulator.sv adds bias-init + buffer select (the matmul result stays in ACC); the LSU owns the ACC → VREG drain (acc.store); new cast on the X-load path.

docs.title: SPAD access model
docs.text:
Locked.

  • Geometry: 32 KiB = 1024 x 256b rows, 8 banks (bank = row mod 8), bf16 storage.
  • Clients: MXU load.w (weight read) and mxu.acc.init (bias read); LSU (vreg.load read, vreg.store write); DMU (devmem <-> SPAD, async). X is now sourced from VREG, so the MXU no longer streams X from SPAD. The VPU does not access SPAD directly - it operates on VREG; SPAD <-> VREG moves go through the LSU.
  • Fixed-latency contract: compute clients (MXU load.w / acc.init, LSU) get a fixed SPAD access latency; bank conflicts among compute clients are compiler-avoided (static scheduling, like VREG).
  • DMU absorbs the arbitration variability: on a bank conflict the DMU yields (it is async and flush-gated, so its latency is already variable / tolerated), so compute never stalls on SPAD. This reuses the existing banked_sram arbiter with priority compute (fixed) > DMU (yields).

Note

SPAD row access latency is RTL-derived (MEM_LATENCY=2 -> about 3 cycles per row) and enters the reservation table as a fixed value; the end-to-end reservation offsets remain TBD.

docs.title: 04 Overlap and roofline
docs.text:
Locked - qualitative bounds only.

  • MXU || VPU overlap: the compiler runs a background nonblocking op (e.g. a matmul) concurrent with a foreground blocking op (e.g. a VPU op) and double-buffers the shared VREG tiles.
  • MLP / matmul-heavy blocks are MXU-bound.
  • Tiny devmem matmuls are DRAM-BW-bound (bounded by the HP-port DRAM bandwidth, not on-chip compute).

FIG. 9 - tile-loop pipeline Gantt for one (m,n) block: weight double-buffer ping-pong (load.w fully hidden), ACC depth-2 tail overlap, steady state 1 matmul / N=8 cy. Canonical home: ISA 7.4. open standalone

Absolute cycle counts and roofline ratios are deliberately omitted here: they are latency-dependent and therefore TBD (RTL-measured) under the sim == synth contract. Only N and the systolic fill 2N-1 are structurally fixed; the VPU depth D_vpu is RTL-measured (currently about 23, measured after the fp-pipeline pass), like L_matmul and the PE pipeline depth.

docs.title: 05 Decisions pending
docs.text:
Still-open items.

Pending · reservation-window offsets

The reservation offsets (d, L_matmul, the MXU PE pipeline depth, the VPU pipeline depth D_vpu, and the absolute numbers in the ISA timing contract) are pending RTL / synth-timing and must be sim == synth verified. D_vpu is an implementation number (currently about 23 (measured after the fp-pipeline pass)), in the same bucket as L_matmul and the PE pipeline depth; only N and the systolic fill 2N-1 are the ISA-visible structurally-fixed values.

Pending · DMU internals

Descriptor slots, burst sizing, dm_axi error / timeout handling, and arbitration are deferred to implementation.

Pending · final sizing

LPM size and base-offset channel count final sizing. The VREG realization is now resolved (whole-tile-row RF + MXU-burst temporal separation; see the locked Functional units section). A 2nd VREG write port remains a sizing option only.

docs.title: Figures
docs.text:
All 40 diagrams from the technical docs, as SVG under docs/mkdocs/figures/tikz/ (how they are generated). Each was generated from its TikZ source via latex + dvisvgm, so the vector geometry and text are the original drawing rather than a screenshot.

Text is drawn as vector outlines, so it renders identically in every browser but is not selectable or searchable.

docs.title: Unreferenced figures
docs.text: These TikZ sources were built and committed, but no LaTeX topic ever included them, so there is no page they belong on. They are embedded here rather than dropped, so the docs/ consolidation loses no figure. Deleting them should be an explicit decision, not an accident of the migration.

docs.title: TikZ figures: source + SVG
docs.text:
The 40 SVGs in this directory are the mkdocs-embeddable form of the diagrams that were drawn in TikZ. They are embedded by the topic pages under ../../reference/ and indexed by ../index.md (the figure gallery).

The .tex next to each .svg is the source of truth. Each NAME.svg is a build product of the NAME.tex beside it, rendered through the recipe in How they are generated below. Edit the TikZ and re-render; do not hand-edit the SVG, or the next re-render silently reverts you.

mininpu-tikz.sty is the shared style (role colours, block styles, \npusep) that all 40 sources \usepackage. It is the only dependency they have: every figure is \documentclass[tikz,border=4pt]{standalone} + \usepackage{mininpu-tikz}.

History. The corpus briefly lived as SVG-only: the sources were at docs/src/figures/*.tex and were deleted wholesale when docs/ became mkdocs-only, leaving 40 outlined SVGs that could not be edited or regenerated. They were recovered from git history and re-homed here, beside the SVG each one produces. The .tex files here are the sole general exception to the "no LaTeX in docs/" rule in CLAUDE.md: they are figure source, never built by mkdocs and never published.

Two further points, both deliberate:

  • Six figures are duplicated into labs/common/figures/ (arch-l1-system, vpu-datapath, vpu-reduce, ct-pingpong, mxu-pe, mxu-array), together with a second copy of mininpu-tikz.sty. The labs/ handouts are still LaTeX and cannot include SVG, so those six are vendored there as source + PDF. The two .tex copies are byte-identical today, but nothing enforces that: if you change one of those six, update all three artifacts (the .tex here, the .tex + .pdf in labs/common/figures/, and the .svg here). See Open: the six-figure duplication.
  • Four figures are not referenced by any topic page (if-addrmap, if-devmem, if-dma-fsm, if-worked). They were built and committed in the LaTeX era but never included. They are embedded in the figure gallery rather than dropped, so removing them stays a decision rather than an accident.

docs.title: How they are generated
docs.text:
The standalone document class crops via pdfTeX page primitives, which do not exist in DVI mode, so dvisvgm's page box lands on the TeX reference point and carries dead space. The build therefore converts via DVI (for correct glyphs) and then crops to the true ink bounding box.

There is no Makefile for this: figures change rarely, and the recipe is three commands. Run it from this directory. Requires latex + dvisvgm (TeX Live) and python3 with cairosvg + pillow (both are in the mininpu conda env).

# 1. TikZ -> DVI  (pgfsys-dvisvgm.def makes pgf emit dvisvgm specials)
export TEXINPUTS=".:$PWD:"
latex -interaction=nonstopmode -output-directory=/tmp/fig \
      "\def\pgfsysdriver{pgfsys-dvisvgm.def}\input{NAME.tex}"

# 2. DVI -> SVG  (outlined glyphs, tight bbox)
dvisvgm --no-fonts --exact -o /tmp/fig/NAME.svg /tmp/fig/NAME.dvi

# 3. crop the viewBox to the rasterized ink bbox + 4pt (matches border=4pt),
#    then install over the committed SVG
python3 crop_viewbox.py /tmp/fig/NAME.svg && cp /tmp/fig/NAME.svg NAME.svg

crop_viewbox.py is committed beside the figures it crops: it is step 3 of this recipe, so it is source like the .tex files are, not prose to copy-paste. It takes one argument (the SVG to crop, rewritten in place), has no options, and reads its PAD = 4.0 / SCALE = 8 constants from the top of the file. Edit it there rather than in this README.

docs.title: What "reproducible" means here
docs.text:
Re-rendering is not byte-identical to the committed SVG, for two benign reasons. Both were confirmed by re-rendering mxu-pe, dm-flow, ct-pingpong and seq-lifecycle against their committed SVGs:

  • <defs> glyph order. dvisvgm emits the glyph <path> defs in an order that depends on its font cache state (a one-shot run orders them differently from a warm batch run). The set of lines is identical: dropping the <svg …> header and sorting the remaining lines gives an identical md5 for all four figures, and the ids are referenced by <use>, so order carries no meaning.
  • Crop quantization. Step 3 measures the ink bbox by rasterizing, so the viewBox depends on SCALE. At SCALE=8 it lands within ~0.2pt of the committed values. Rendering both under the same header is pixel-exact (0 differing pixels of ~1.9M at 1600px wide), so the residual is the crop measurement, not content.

So: verify a re-render by pixel-comparing, not by cmp. A re-render whose sorted body matches and whose viewBox is within a fraction of a point is correct.

docs.title: Decisions worth not re-litigating
docs.text:
• DVI route, not PDF route. dvisvgm --pdf on a pdflatex-built PDF crops correctly but silently drops the \npusep middot glyphs (dm-flow: 637 vs 648 glyph <use> elements). The DVI route keeps every glyph.
  • No svgo. It cuts 3.7M to 2.2M but drops group stroke colour declarations on some figures (mxu-pe #5a5a5a, if-devmem #d55e00). The size win is not worth unverifiable colour changes.

docs.title: Open: --no-fonts costs searchable text
docs.text:
All 40 committed SVGs have a <text> count of 0: --no-fonts outlines every glyph into <path> + <use>. Figure labels are therefore not selectable, not greppable, and invisible to screen readers. This is a real accessibility and searchability cost, and now that the sources are back it is a live choice rather than a fait accompli.

Measured on mxu-pe and seq-lifecycle (dvisvgm 3.2.1):

figure --no-fonts (committed) --font-format=woff2 mxu-pe <text> 0, <use> 300, 75065 B <text> 56, <use> 0, 28676 B (-62%) seq-lifecycle <text> 0, <use> 303, 76483 B <text> 46, <use> 0, 27135 B (-65%)

The fonts are embedded, not referenced. The woff2 output carries 8 (mxu-pe) and 5 (seq-lifecycle) @font-face blocks, each with a src:url(data:application /x-font-woff2;base64,…) payload and zero external url(http…) references. So "the CM fonts are not installed on the reader's machine" is not a failure mode here: the SVG stays self-contained.

The actual failure mode is renderer text layout. Rendered through cairosvg (the only SVG renderer installed here), the woff2 variant is legible but visibly wrong: inter-word spacing collapses (weight in -> weightin, activation in -> activationin), subscripts misplace, and bold weight is lost. dvisvgm positions each text run at explicit x/y and relies on the renderer honouring the embedded font's advance widths; cairosvg does not, so the layout drifts. Outlined text has no layout to get wrong and renders identically in every engine.

This does not mean woff2 is broken in browsers, which is where the docs are actually read. It is the same gap the original conversion flagged and it is still open: no browser and no second renderer (rsvg-convert, resvg, chromium, firefox) is installed in this environment, so "woff2 renders correctly in a real browser" remains unverified in both directions. The tradeoff:

  • --no-fonts (status quo): renders identically everywhere; 2.7x larger; text is not selectable, greppable, or screen-readable.
  • --font-format=woff2: 2.7x smaller, real <text>, self-contained fonts; correctness depends on the renderer honouring embedded-font metrics, confirmed wrong under cairosvg and unconfirmed under any browser.

To settle it, render one figure both ways and open both in Chrome and Firefox. If woff2 is clean in both, switching is a strict win and worth doing for all 40.

docs.title: Open: the six-figure duplication
docs.text:
arch-l1-system, vpu-datapath, vpu-reduce, ct-pingpong, mxu-pe and mxu-array now exist as .tex in two places (here and labs/common/figures/), as does mininpu-tikz.sty (7 duplicated files, currently byte-identical). labs/ needs LaTeX+PDF; docs/ needs SVG; nothing keeps the two .tex copies in sync, so the next edit to one of the six can silently skew the handout from the docs page. Options, for the owner to pick:

  1. Canonical here, symlink from labs/. labs/common/figures/NAME.tex -> ../../../docs/mkdocs/figures/tikz/NAME.tex. Zero build changes; relies on symlinks surviving checkout (fine on Linux/macOS CI, breaks on Windows and in some archive exports).
  2. Canonical here, copy at build time. A labs/ Makefile prerequisite copies the six .tex + the .sty before latex runs. Robust and explicit; adds a generated-file rule and a .gitignore entry to labs/.
  3. Canonical here, TEXINPUTS instead of copying. Point the labs/ build at this directory via TEXINPUTS and delete the vendored .tex. No copies, no symlinks; couples the labs/ build path to a docs/ directory layout.
  4. Third location (figures/ at repo root) that both consume. Cleanest conceptually, largest diff, and moves 40 files for the sake of 6.
  5. Keep the duplication, add a CI drift gate that diffs the two copies and fails on skew. Smallest change; keeps the duplication but makes it loud.

A drift gate (5) is worth having under any of 1-4 that leaves two copies, and is the cheapest immediate protection. This note is a proposal only: no consolidation has been done, and labs/common/figures/ is untouched.

docs.title: Labs
docs.text:
Ported verbatim; technical claims under review

These handouts were ported from their LaTeX sources content-preserving: the prose, listings, figures, and exercises are carried over as written, with no rewrite. Their technical claims have not been re-validated against the current RTL and ISA and are under review, tracked in issue #385. Treat module names, instruction mnemonics, and datapath descriptions on these pages as handout text, not as a reference for the hardware as it stands today. The authoritative sources are the ISA Reference and the ABI Register Map.

The labs build a small neural-network accelerator from the inside out, one subsystem per lab. Start with Lab 0, which covers the accelerator, its programming model, the toolchain, and the submission workflow every later lab assumes.

Lab Handout Subject 0 Course Introduction and Setup The accelerator, its programming model, the five-lab arc, the toolchain, and grading 1 The Vector Processing Unit An eight-lane dot product, a tile loop, per-lane arithmetic and activations, a complete layer 2 The Matrix Unit A processing element, the systolic fabric, running alongside the vector unit, capturing results 3 The Data-Movement Engine Single-tile transfers, multi-dimensional addressing, double-buffering, the complete accelerator 4 Writing Kernels with Triton A tiled matrix multiply, tiled attention with online softmax, the causal mask, compiling and running 5 Serving Cached decode, a paged cache, allocator and scheduler, offload to host memory

docs.title: Lab 0: Course Introduction and Setup
docs.text:
ECE 4950/6950 AI Hardware, Fall 2026

School of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Cornell University

Lab 0: Course Introduction and Setup

Due: TBD, Fall 2026

Late submission: 4% penalty per day; cannot be late by more than 6 days.

Ported verbatim; technical claims under review

This handout was ported from its LaTeX source content-preserving: the prose, listings, figures, and exercises are carried over as written, with no rewrite. Its technical claims have not been re-validated against the current RTL and ISA and are under review, tracked in issue #385. Treat module names, instruction mnemonics, and datapath descriptions on this page as handout text, not as a reference for the hardware as it stands today. The authoritative sources are the ISA Reference and the ABI Register Map.

On this page specifically: the instruction reference below, and the kernel walkthroughs that use it, are written in a vec.* / matmul.tile / dma.* / cast.* mnemonic namespace that matches no generation of the ISA. The generated ISA Reference is the authority on what mnemonics exist and what they do.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: These labs build a small neural-network accelerator from the inside out. The datapath you build is a general tensor engine, but the workload it targets, and that motivates its design, is decoder language-model inference: by the end you will have written the hardware for a working tensor accelerator, run real language-model computations on it, and served a model with it. This document covers the parts that every lab assumes: what the accelerator is and how a host program drives it, what each lab adds, how to set up the toolchain on the ecelinux servers, and how you submit and are graded. Read it once before Lab 1. Each lab handout points back here rather than repeating it.

docs.title: The accelerator and its programming model
docs.text:
The accelerator is not a CPU. It does not fetch your program, manage a stack, or run an operating system. It is an IO-attached device: a separate piece of hardware that a host computer talks to over a bus, in the same way a host talks to a disk or a network card. You will see it described as the mininpu (Neural Processing Unit). The host runs ordinary software (in these labs, a Python script); the device runs only the fixed-function hardware you build.

Figure 1

The host and the accelerator. The host copies tensors into device memory, writes a control register to launch a kernel, and copies results back. The device is a fixed datapath with its own on-chip memory; it never runs the host's software. Lab 1 works entirely in simulation, so think of the host as the test program and the device as the hardware you are building.

A program drives the device in three steps.

  • Copy data in. The host copies input tensors (the model weights and the activations) from its own memory into the device's memory with a block copy, a memcpy. Nothing is computed yet; the data is simply moved to where the hardware can reach it.
  • Launch a kernel. A kernel is a short program written in the device's own assembly, the NPU instruction set. The host hands the device a kernel and signals it to start (in hardware terms, it writes a control register). The device executes the kernel against the data now resident in its memory. The host does not step through the kernel; it launches it and waits for a done signal. (Writing kernel text by hand is only needed in these labs for tiny examples; later labs introduce a compiler that generates it.)
  • Copy results out. When the kernel finishes, the host copies the output tensors back from device memory into its own memory.

This is the whole interface: copy in, launch a kernel, copy out.

A kernel is a short sequence of NPU instructions. The hardware fetches each instruction in turn, decodes it to decide which units act and on which data, and executes it on the tensors in device memory. The instructions are simple and specific to the work: there is one that sums the elements of a row down to a single value (vec.rowsum), one that multiplies two vectors element by element (vec.mul.acc), one that applies a nonlinear activation such as GELU (vec.gelu), and, in the matrix unit, one that performs a tiled matrix multiply. The set of instructions the hardware understands, together with exactly what each one does, is its instruction set. The labs build the hardware units that fetch, decode, and execute these instructions: Lab 1 builds the unit that runs the vector instructions, Lab 2 the one that runs the matrix-multiply instruction, and Lab 3 the one that runs the instructions that move tensors in and out of the scratchpad.

Two properties of the device shape everything you build.

A fixed, specialized datapath. A CPU has general-purpose units that can run any instruction in any order. The accelerator instead wires up a fixed set of arithmetic units in the exact shape that neural-network math needs: lanes of multipliers and adders, reduction trees, and table-lookup units for nonlinear functions. Data streams through this datapath. There is no general-purpose instruction stream rerouting work at run time; the structure is committed in the hardware. This is what makes the device fast and small for its one job, and inflexible for anything else.

A scratchpad, not a cache. A CPU hides memory latency with caches: hardware automatically decides what to keep on chip, and your program is unaware of it. The accelerator has no cache. Its on-chip memory is a scratchpad (also called the SPM or SPAD), a plain addressable RAM that the program manages explicitly. Data is on chip only because the program copied it there, and it stays exactly where the program put it. Nothing moves on its own. This trades the convenience of a cache for predictability: because movement is explicit, the cost of every access is known in advance, which is what lets the hardware run a tight, overlapped schedule. "Data lives where you put it" is the mental model to carry through every lab.

docs.title: What you build across the five labs
docs.text:
The labs build one accelerator step by step. Each lab leaves you with a larger, working subsystem; nothing built earlier is thrown away or replaced. The first three labs build the hardware; the last two program it.

  • Lab 1: the Vector Processing Unit (VPU). The VPU does the element-wise and vector math: multiply, add, the reductions that sum a row of numbers, and the activation functions. You build it in simulation and finish by running a small neural-network layer through it. At the end of Lab 1 you have a complete VPU.
  • Lab 2: the Matrix Unit (MXU). The MXU is a systolic array that does matrix multiplication, the bulk of the work in a transformer (cf. the systolic arrays in NVIDIA Tensor Cores and the Google TPU). You add it alongside the VPU and run on the FPGA board. At the end of Lab 2 you have a VPU and an MXU.
  • Lab 3: the data-movement engine (DMA). The DMA moves tensors between off-chip DRAM and the on-chip scratchpad, and overlaps that movement with computation. Adding it completes the accelerator. At the end of Lab 3 you have a VPU, an MXU, and a DMA: a full accelerator able to run decoder language-model inference on the board.
  • Lab 4: writing kernels. With the hardware finished, you write compute kernels for it using Triton, a Python-based language for accelerator kernels (cf. Triton and FlashAttention), and a compiler that lowers them to the device's assembly. You implement attention kernels and run them on the accelerator you built.
  • Lab 5: serving. Finally you build the software layer that serves a model: managing the key-value cache so that generating each new token reuses past work instead of recomputing it (cf. the paged attention used in vLLM and SGLang).

Lab 1 runs its hardware in simulation at a small scale and in FP32, so the work is getting the logic right; Labs 2 and 3 run on the board at full model scale, and Lab 2 is where the lower-precision storage that real inference uses comes in. Each subsystem is the production design; what differs between Lab 1 and the board labs is the scale it runs at and the number format it stores, not the design.

docs.title: The worked model: GPT-2
docs.text:
These labs run GPT-2 small (124M parameters): 12 decoder layers, 12 attention heads, a model width of 768, and a feed-forward width of 3072. As a decoder it generates one token at a time, each token attending only to earlier positions.

One layer is two residual sublayers, each preceded by a LayerNorm (the pre-LayerNorm arrangement): multi-head self-attention, then a feed-forward block with a GELU activation. A forward pass embeds the input token and its position, runs the stack of layers, applies a final LayerNorm, and projects to vocabulary logits with a head whose weights are the (tied) input-embedding matrix. The token and position embedding lookups and the final argmax run on the host; all the arithmetic in between, including the layer matmuls and that logit projection, runs on the device these labs build. These handouts are not a transformer tutorial: this is the map, and each lab builds or programs one part of it. The matrix multiplications (the projections, the attention scores, the feed-forward) are the bulk of the work and are what the matrix unit accelerates; the LayerNorms, the softmax inside attention, and GELU are vector work; the key-value cache is what makes every token after the first cheap. Lab 1 first builds the vector unit on a smaller, self-contained example, a small MLP over sklearn's load_digits 8×8 handwritten digits (classes 0 through 7), a compact stand-in for the MNIST-MLP concept.

docs.title: The toolchain
docs.text:
The course uses a small set of tools, introduced as you need them. The first labs build hardware and test it in simulation; the later labs program the finished hardware. The tools, and where each is used:

  • Python: tests, numpy and torch reference oracles, kernel scripts.
  • Verilator: open-source RTL simulator that compiles your SystemVerilog for simulation (Labs 1 to 3).
  • cocotb: Python testbench framework that drives the simulation and defines the public tests (Labs 1 to 3).
  • Surfer: waveform viewer for inspecting VCD dumps while debugging (Labs 1 to 3).
  • The NPU assembler and instruction set: kernels are short programs in the NPU ISA; the assembler encodes them (Labs 1 to 3).
  • The cost model (Lab 4): estimates a kernel's cycle count from its instruction mix, so you can reason about performance (for example, the memory-traffic benefit of the attention kernel) without a board run.
  • Vivado: synthesis and bitstream generation for the FPGA (Labs 2 to 3).
  • The ZCU102 board: the Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ FPGA the accelerator runs on (Labs 2 to 5).
  • mininpu-compiler: lowers Triton kernels to the NPU instruction set (Lab 4).
  • Triton: the language you write accelerator kernels in (Lab 4).
  • mininpu-runtime: the host-side driver that copies data to the device and launches kernels from Python (Labs 2 to 5).

Detailed tutorials for these tools are provided separately and are linked from the course page (to be added).

docs.title: Setup: the ecelinux servers and git
docs.text:
The toolchain (the simulator, the cocotb test framework, and the synthesis tools used in the FPGA labs) is pre-installed on the ecelinux servers. You do your work there over ssh; you do not install anything locally. Each lab is distributed as a per-student git repository in the course GitHub organization, with the starter code already in it.

To start a lab, log in, load the course environment, clone your repository for that lab, and run the tests once to confirm everything builds:

ssh <netid>@ecelinux.ece.cornell.edu
source /classes/ece4950/setup.sh
git clone git@github.com:ece4950-fa26/ece4950-labN-<netid>.git
cd ece4950-labN-<netid>
make test

The first command logs you into the servers. The second loads the course toolchain into your shell; run it once per login session. The third clones your copy of lab N (replace N with the lab number and <netid> with your NetID). The last command builds the design and runs the public test suite. On a fresh checkout the build succeeds and the tests report failures, because the parts you are meant to write are still empty. As you complete the lab, the tests turn from failing to passing.1

docs.title: Submission and grading
docs.text:
Submission is through git. You submit by committing your work and pushing it to the main branch of your repository. Pushing is submitting; there is no separate upload step and nothing to hand in on any other system. The latest commit on main at the deadline is what is graded. At the deadline, the course staff place an immutable git tag on your final commit; that tag is the official graded snapshot. Do not move or delete it, and do not rewrite the history it points to. The late policy stated on each lab handout is enforced out of band.

Grading has two parts. The first is correctness, measured by a public cocotb test suite that runs automatically when you push. These are the same tests you run locally with make test, under the same pinned tool versions, so the result you see on your machine is the result you are graded on. There is no hidden test tier. The second part is a short written report (about one page) analyzing a design trade-off specific to the lab; each handout says what to address. Include the report as report.pdf in your repository.

docs.title: The NPU instruction set: a kernel reference
docs.text:
The rest of this document is a reference for the instructions themselves. The sections above explain the programming model (the host copies data in, launches a kernel, and copies results out); this part lists the specific operations a GPT-2 inference kernel uses, and for each one explains what it does to the data. You build the hardware that runs these instructions across Labs 1 to 3: the vector operations in Lab 1, the matrix multiply in Lab 2, and the data-movement operations in Lab 3. Keep this section open while you work; it is a standing reference, not a problem set, so it has no exercises of its own.

A forward pass has two phases. Prefill runs the whole prompt through the model at once: the rows of a tile are the prompt tokens, so a tile carries M > 1 tokens. Decode then generates one token at a time, so a tile carries a single row. Both phases run the same instructions; prefill simply has more than one row per tile. Where the row count matters (the LayerNorm affine, in particular) the text says so.

The full accelerator understands more instructions than the ones here, including variants for lower-precision and quantized formats and for richer addressing. This reference is deliberately a subset: the operations a GPT-2 inference kernel actually issues, and nothing else. The last subsection, What this reference leaves out, names what is omitted and why, so the boundary is explicit.

docs.title: Where the data lives
docs.text:
Every instruction reads and writes one of three on-chip stores. You need the distinction to read the operation table.

  • The scratchpad (SPM). The plain addressable on-chip RAM described earlier, holding bfloat16 (bf16) tiles. Weights and activations are copied here from off-chip memory before a kernel runs. A tile is one row of eight bf16 values.
  • The accumulator (ACC). The same physical on-chip memory viewed as 32-bit floating-point (fp32) tiles. Matrix multiply writes its result here, and the vector arithmetic operates here, so that intermediate sums keep fp32 precision. A row is eight fp32 values.
  • The vector registers (VREG). A small set of fp32 registers, each holding eight values. The reductions write their per-row result here (one value per row), and the per-row and per-feature scalars used by the broadcast operations are read from here.

The pattern throughout a kernel is: copy bf16 tiles into the scratchpad, widen them to fp32 in the accumulator, compute in fp32, narrow the fp32 result back to bf16, and copy it out. The three cast operations move data between the bf16 and fp32 views.

This reference describes the general 16-bit-store, fp32-accumulate kernel shape. The labs reach it in two steps. Lab 1 stays fp32 end to end (so the work is getting the logic right, not the numerics). Lab 2 switches the 16-bit storage format to BF16: the same matmul.tile op with only the storage format changed, so the kernel structure above is unaffected.

docs.title: The instruction subset
docs.text: Instruction What it does in a kernel Used by matmul.tile Multiplies two tiles, accumulating in fp32. Flags select transpose of the second operand, accumulate-into-destination, and a causal mask. Every GEMM: QKV projections, attention scores, attention output, output projection, the two MLP layers, the logit projection. dma.load.spm Copies a bf16 tensor from off-chip memory into the scratchpad. Loading weights and activations for a tile. dma.load.acc Copies an fp32 tensor from off-chip memory into the accumulator. Loading a bias vector. dma.store.spm Copies a bf16 tile from the scratchpad back to off-chip memory. Writing a layer output the host will read. cast.load Widens a bf16 scratchpad row to an fp32 accumulator row. Bringing a loaded tile into the fp32 compute domain. cast.store Narrows an fp32 accumulator row to a bf16 scratchpad row. Preparing a result to store or to feed the next matmul. cast.load.vreg Widens a 1-D bf16 scratchpad row (shape 1 × N) into an fp32 vector register of length N. Loading a 1-D weight vector (the LayerNorm γ) into a register for a following vec.scale.col.bcast. vec.zero.acc Sets an accumulator tile to zero.
